story:
I’d been putting off our re-registration paperwork for a month. With Sara in Mexico and the deadline looming, I finally booked a counselor. The waiting room felt like a dentist’s office—old magazines and fake plants—fitting for a process as routine and clinical as renewing a relationship license. It wasn’t that I couldn’t see another year with Sara; we’d settled into a warm, comfortable rhythm. But each anniversary turned into a quiet referendum: hire a lawyer, wait at the DMV, sit through the government’s 15-minute interview—and decide whether to continue. The logistics weren’t the true stressor. The idea of a scheduled stopping point was.

The counselor suggested a data consultancy, the kind the government covered under universal healthcare to help people make better relationship decisions. Sara and I had always been old-fashioned about that stuff, but I went. The consultant walked me through the research: relationships are the biggest drivers of happiness—bigger than money or career—and most start by chance and persist by inertia. Longitudinal studies showed that roughly 80% of close relationships formed randomly, and unhealthy romantic ones dragged on for an average of 7.3 years before ending.

Then he pulled up our prediction. Their model, trained on nearly a billion couples, had millions of cases similar to us—late twenties, Asian male/Latina female, fourth re-registration. On average, those relationships lasted 1.8 more years after the fourth renewal; the median was 0.8. He emailed me our personalized outcome distribution and offered the next step: scan our social media data to find highly compatible partners nearby, compare their scores against Sara’s, and decide.

I tuned out. I’d read the papers linking high compatibility scores to relationship longevity—and the rebuttals that said those scores just boosted confidence, which then sustained the relationship. My mind landed on something simple: compatibility isn’t static. It grows. Over time, Sara and I had been converging—me going to church with her sometimes, her picking up my favorite jazz. Maybe you don’t find “the most compatible” person; maybe you become them over time if the relationship is healthy. I left the office smiling, ready to start the paperwork.

That’s when reality intervened. When Sara got back, she was firm: no re-registration. She’d seen a counselor and a consultant too, and her conclusion differed from mine. “Jack, I promise you this will be best for both of us. You won’t know it, but I’ll be praying for you. You’ll meet Kate on the twenty-third date. Thirty years from then, you’ll still be together.” Those were her last words to me.

A year later, I went back to the consultants. They found 23 “extremely compatible” matches nearby. On date 23, I met Kate. It’s been 30 years, and we’ve never looked back.

Along the way, the economic story behind all this started to make sense. In the 1970s, behavioral economists showed that tiny frictions—like changing a menu price—can disproportionately shape decisions. Governments later tested how friction could improve well-being. Re-registration forces a choice. It interrupts inertia. Back in the old days, we floated until crisis pushed us to act. Now, Kate and I have to actively choose each other, again and again. We don’t get complacent because the structure won’t let us.

I still believe what clicked that summer: given a happy relationship and enough time, you become the best possible partners for each other. But that hinges on honesty about whether your relationship is truly happy. I was honest. So was Sara. Honesty just took us in different directions.
